lines.text: "Daydreaming. That's a luxury you can't afford in the Delta. Here, you'd better pay attention to what's paying attention to you. Yes, even the plants. Especially the plants." Welcome to the jungle.

Phosphorescent moss, gramophone plants that moan and shriek in the spore wind. The vurnshafts, which is essentially a living tunnel with throbbing veins and organs that puff hallucinogenic gas at you. Deeply unsettling.

The chapter weaponizes boredom against both the characters and the reader. Farley's warnings seem "hollow," at first. A recruit mistakes a branch for a snake. Nyland lulls you into thinking maybe the Delta isn't so bad.
And then the ground just... stops making sense. Trees hanging sideways, roots torn out, and they find this subsurface void. The transition from mundane to nightmarish is so fast.
The chapter trains you to distrust calm the same way the Delta trains its inhabitants. By the time we're underground and that membrane goes "violently porous," the jungle truly wakes up.
Kirsten knows exactly what to do: unstrap, cover, connect oxygen. But she was already too late. Her training is perfect and it still fails her.

That's the cruel thesis of this chapter, isn't it? Competence isn't enough. Kirsten is clearly experienced, her squad is prepared, they have Scitters doing reconnaissance, heated shovels, atmospheric readers. They have technology. And the Delta just... doesn't care.

And then we get that psychedelic vision sequence—the "crystal world" and "geometric light"—which I desperately want more information about. Farley comes out of it touching the mycelium "with newfound wonder."

I think Farley might already be compromised. The text makes a point of how she moves—"lightly, instinctively choosing firmer ground." She belongs here in a way the guardians don't. And after the gas, she's the one kneeling in wonder while everyone else is groaning on the ground.
You think she's been changed by the Delta before this mission?
I think the Delta and Farley have an understanding that Kirsten doesn't have access to yet. Whether that's good or sinister, I can't tell.

What wrecked me was Nasr. This poor recruit drifts just a few steps off the path during the spore storm, and the bog just... pulled him under. Kirsten tries to save him and nearly gets consumed herself.
That image of her reaching for him while Farley is screaming at her to let go. That's the moment Kirsten has to choose between being a hero and being alive.
And she doesn't really choose! Farley drags her back. The chapter keeps stripping agency from these characters.
Which brings us to that ending. Inside what appears to be the remains of something enormous. Leathery egg husks. Gelatinous fluid. Cartilage that still has give.

But back up a bit. Here's what I'm left wondering: that entity Kirsten glimpsed during the gas exposure, the one showing her "geometric light"—was that the Delta trying to kill her, or trying to communicate?
And if Farley came out of that same vision looking enlightened instead of traumatized... what did she understand that Kirsten didn't? I want to keep reading immediately.
